stem: In a employee training study comparing Training Program X vs. Y: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher performance improvement (66.7% vs 57.3%). However, when stratified by initial skill level: In junior employees (n_X=30, n_Y=120): Treatment X achieves 79% vs Treatment Y's 73%. In senior employees (n_X=80, n_Y=30): Treatment X achieves 49% vs Treatment Y's 42%. The true population proportion of junior employees is uncertain, estimated between 37% and 51%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to senior employees (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.065, 0.066], point estimate: 0.066. Treatment X is genuinely better by 6.6 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=57.3%, Y=66.7% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In junior employees: X=79% > Y=73%. In senior employees: X=49% > Y=42%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Training Program X is applied more to senior employees (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
Not considering that additional unobserved confounders could further modify the comparison
